sentences.text: Well, I'm David Shear.
Previous, David Sukaczer.
And I'm-- I was born September 17, in 1919.
I was born in Poland.
And it was the Polish Ukraine.
And the name of the city was Luboml.
In Yiddish, Libivne.
You mean, before the war?
Well, it was a long stretch from Libivne to where the-- I met the-- the Germans.
I left Libivne when I was a youngster, when I finished my preliminary schooling.
And so I left for the big city, to go to high school or further education.
And through-- I ended up-- from one city to another, from one school to another.
And I ended up to be in central Poland, in Ostrowiec.
Ostrowca was a [BACKGROUND NOISE] different city than Luboml was.
Luboml was more of the Russian-style Jews.
Ostrowca was a Hasidic city, where the Jews were wearing long caftans and beards, and the-- the style of the Jewishness was different than the Ukrainian Jews.
When I came to Ostrowca, which it was in 1935, I-- I came there because the-- the school-- at the time, they were called the yeshiva-- sent me.
When you graduated one school, they send you to a higher school.
And they were all under the same name.
Like it was the same type of schooling.
And my type of yeshiva was-- it was more of a-- [NON-ENGLISH] built on-- based on Musar.
We did not-- our education was not to become a professional.
It was to stay in the educated-- in the educational business, [BACKGROUND NOISE] whatever today you would call it.
But at the time, it was to dedicate your life for Jewish culture and to continue living-- to continue to spread the Jewish culture.
So I stayed in this-- in this yeshiva from '35.
And, of course, I continued.
And the Germans came out-- came into the city.
They occupied in 1939.
It was my 20th birthday.
And this was my gift, when  the Germans came in in 1939.
And the day when they arrived, they occupied the city.
Well, automatically everybody got scared, and people started to-- to disappear, to hide.
The yeshiva stopped existing, because we were afraid to concentrate as Jews, because we heard that they-- they are not too friendly to this type of people.
And then, the next day, we right away faced problems.
They used to catch you in the street.
If they saw anybody walking in the street, they just took-- took him away, just to work for the day, or for two days.
Or sometimes they took him for a week.
Just to-- I think, even, they even pay you, in the beginning, because it was a city project.
Well, I-- if I'll tell you, I'll-- I'm going to talk about Luboml, because I was a youngster, and everything was-- I left there when I was 12 years old.
And it was really not too much of a social life there, because I was too young for those things.
But most my life, socially, started developing in Ostrowca.
And there was no-- it's very hard to describe.
Today, anybody who doesn't drive or doesn't write on Shabbat-- on the Jewish Shabbos, as they call it-- or anybody who doesn't smoke a cigarette on-- on Shabbat is considered a religious person.
But this was not the case in Europe.
In Europe, you had to be more than just not to smoke or not to go to work.
You've got to be dedicated to Jewishness.
So to ask, how was the holidays, holidays was the thing people were living for.
This was the only entertainment.
The only pleasure they got out of life [BACKGROUND NOISE] was these holidays, these gathering of people, and to celebrate together with friends or with relatives.
So it was a way of life.
It was not just a-- a tradition, like we-- we know it today, that we observe this or we observe that.
We were looking forward for these holidays.
And it was a-- the biggest part of the Jewish life.
What was your experience of anti-Semitism before the war?
Yeah.
This is a very-- I suppose it is known, but people don't talk about it.
My first [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] problem with-- with physical-- physical problem with-- from-- which I-- occurred to me was not from the Germans but from the Poles.
I had my nose broken, walking in the street at the wrong time.
Because I had to go a little in the dark, so people shouldn't see me, from the Germans.
But I didn't think that a Pole will pass by.
And he didn't like me because he thought-- he knew he was-- I was Jewish.
Then he just broke my nose, for no reason at all.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Well, this might-- might have been a-- one incident that happened to me.
But it also happened to-- to the system.
After the occupation, the Germans did not know the difference between a Pole and a Jew.
It is the Poles who let them in, the Poles who told them that this is a Jew and this is not a Jew.
It is unfortunate if this sounds a little too-- there are certain things in life that-- it is true, but you don't talk about it.
And it's very little publicized, but that was the truth.
The Poles-- we heard the same things about the Russians as we did the Americans that knew the Russians after the war.
So it's very, very uncomfortable even to talk about.
Which today, they are-- everybody's considered friends, and we are trying to play up to these-- to countries which we don't want to know their past.
Well, it is very unfortunate.
Unfortunately, I was left alone.
I was always alone, since I was-- I left my-- my parents died on me when I was a youngster.
And when I came to this-- to Ostrowca, because the school-- the-- At-- at the time, it was the [? dayan. ?] [?
Dayan, ?] it was.
The one who was in charge-- the principal of the school.
He knew that I had no parents, and I would end up [BACKGROUND NOISE] doing like every Jew was doing there, doing their work.
And he thought that I deserved something better, because I was quite a-- a different, smart little boy.
Maybe they just liked me.
So he gave me a letter of recommendation, and I went to the big city.
And, from this day on, I was on my own.
I-- in the beginning, you know, I ate every day in a different house.
I slept with-- with strange places.
I-- many times I had to sleep at the-- at the school, because there was no room for me to sleep except on a bench.
So to say who was with me, I would say that-- that God was with me, most of the time.
And that's the way I felt, since I was a youngster.
Somehow, I felt that-- that something was-- somebody was guiding you.
And today, we think of it as a prayer.
If we talk about-- and a high power takes-- watches over us, or whatever it is.
But sometimes, when you live your life and you see things happen that-- nobody has control over it, except, you think, yourself, and you know you are very helpless.
So you feel that you're never-- never alone, and this was my strength when I was a youngster, too.
So I really had nobody except my feelings, that my-- conviction that somebody is guiding me.
Yes.
I've never-- I haven't mentioned-- when I was born-- which it was-- I already had-- my father remarried my mother.
And I-- my father had a son and a daughter from his previous marriage.
So, all of a sudden, I remember when I was four that my brother was already married.
He was around 20 years old.
And my sister, which it was a half-sister-- she was maybe a year younger or maybe two years younger.
And it's a matter of fact, when I-- when my mother died, when I was three months old, because she had pneumonia.
And in Poland it was a deadly disease, if you don't-- even for young people.
So my sister raised me.
Of course, it was-- the father left the house.
So-- and my sister and them-- my half-sister and brother, they lived in this house, and his family.
So I would say that my sister raised me.
But when I left this house, when I left Luboml, I lost all connections with my brother, because my brother was not exactly very happy that I left home.
He thought I would stay and help him with his work or whatever his business was.
By the way, he was-- you wouldn't believe it, but the profession my-- my brother took over from my father-- he used to make ropes.
He used to make-- Like, in Poland, they-- they-- they-- they didn't have-- the-- the mechanism wasn't-- weren't mechanized.
So everything was horse and-- horses and buggies.
So the horses had to pull the wagon with two ropes on the side tied onto the wagon.
And this was a quite a popular business.
Oh, they used to-- also used to make ropes for-- those heavy ropes to catch-- to pull the nets, to catch fish.
And this was a very complicated or popular business.
And my brother took over this business.
And he was hoping that I would continue with him.
And I hated this thing, because it was always work outside in-- in 30 below zero, sometimes.
And it was very-- And-- and, of course, when I got home from school, and the next day, you know, I-- I told my principal that I couldn't, you know-- why I didn't do my homework, maybe.
Maybe I wasn't as sharp as I-- he expected me.
I told him that I had to help my brother with his-- excuse me-- blasted kind of work, which it was very uncomfortable, especially for a youngster.
So they didn't have too much problem to convince me to leave this place.
So the only thing-- my sister, which she raised me-- she got married, and she moved to the next city.
As I said, it was next to Chelm, which everybody knows about the Chelm Jewish folklore stories comes of jokes.
Chelm was a very lovely city.
To me, if I look back, Chelm was like the San Francisco of-- of the United States.
It was a lovely city, and very much full of education and life.
And-- so my sister moved-- lived there, and that's the only place I used to pass by, to see my sister, because she was not well off.
And she had a very big pleasure to see me growing up and the way I was.
And so she was very happy.
So-- and this was-- Actually, I was never in contact with these people before-- before the war, and even so after the war.
I mean, we never met.
So I left them when I was 35.
The only time I used to see my sister is once in a while.
Well, they-- my brother did not survive.
Because it was-- they lived in the border-- right when-- when the war between the Russians and German broke out, they were right under firing line.
And when they came in, they were not prepared.
When the Germans came in, they just slaughtered them all.
The whole everybody.
Nobody was left.
But my sister, she was at a border on the Polish side.
And when she came-- she wanted to escape over the border.
The Russians wouldn't let her stay there, so they sent her to Siberia, with her family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- and this was, at the time, it was the worst thing that could ever happen to anybody.
But it turned out, this way, this was the only way she survived, because she did not stay on the firing line when the Germans crossed the border, because she went to-- to Siberia.
And later, after the war, I looked for any survivors, and I find my sister, that she was in one of the displaced persons' camps.
So-- and I met my sister, and she and her husband and children.
So she was the only one who survived.
And, of course, through circumstances, my sister went to Israel.
And she started a new life in Israel with her family.
No, I did not know anybody who cared for me or anybody who would-- would claim me as any connection with my life.
And I-- when I was young, I never thought of it.
I never-- I never even saw-- had a picture of my father or my mother.
I don't even know how they look like.
I might walk in the street and see the same face which my mother looked like, but I wouldn't-- wouldn't know.
So-- what was your question, again?
It was-- it's just like you-- you-- you grow up, and you don't [BACKGROUND NOISE] you don't know any different.
You don't know-- like, not to compare, it is like when you see somebody who was born with one arm.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] To us, this man is handicapped.
But to somebody who has never had a-- he only lived-- he was born with one arm, to them it's normal.
Or a blind person.
If you get-- God forbid, you lose an eye, you think is the world is coming to an end.
But people who never experienced the pleasure, or  the-- the-- the-- to be normal, to have both eyes and all-- to them it's-- you-- you-- you get used to it.
And it is a part of life.
So to be an orphan-- I never asked why everybody has beautiful outfits for the holidays and I had to wear a second-hand pair of pants or-- or outfit.
I mean, this was the way life is.
Everyone-- people look  different, and people live different.
And can-- one cannot claim that he's entitled just because somebody else has it.
So it's very difficult--
Now, entirely, it's a different system.
Today, people-- everything is-- is-- people think different.
They're commercialized.
And everything is-- that you-- you're entitled to everything.
But not in my days.
In my days, you learned to be happy with the things that you have.
As long as you were able to enjoy it, you were well, and you didn't have a terrible cold or you didn't have a terrible sickness.
So it was normal to grow up and to-- As a matter of fact, you had to behave, because you were depending on other people.
Not on relatives, or not on people who would tolerate certain things.
So you didn't wait just to be told.
You knew you had to behave as a normal boy, as a normal human being.
That's all what we have.
We had-- we-- we became-- we went to school-- most of the-- of the boys who came to these schools, they came from out of town, from different-- different cities.
Small cities.
They sent their-- their boys there to get their education at a big-- at a big school.
So we developed a closeness, because the-- the studying over there was not just to make the grade.
It was not just to-- to-- to pass the grade.

It's a matter of fact, our-- if I were to describe the type of schools we had, when I was, remember, 15, we practiced-- we practiced-- today, I would call it 'transcendental meditation.' And we practiced-- when I was 15, already, the type of education we had-- it was-- it was transcendental meditation, but with a religious motive, not just with, like, nonsense to keep on repeating and to keep on killing your-- your-- your brain or killing your-- not to think of anything, just to have your mind blank.
No, we had to-- we practiced transcendental meditation with a reason, that-- that things are not-- to-- to assure you that things are not done just because it is-- it is there.
Everything has a reason, and everything has-- has a-- a-- a plan for you.
And this was a very strong way of building youngsters.
So those youngsters, they-- we were all friends.
We all talked our-- to each other like brothers.
Because most of the time, we all needed each other, because they went away from their home and they missed probably their sisters and brothers, and neither were in the yeshiva, in the school.
We were together, having-- facing the same problems.
So those were my best friends.
And, of course, the teachers, they were enormous people.
They were people-- we used to call them-- they were-- [SPEAKING HEBREW].
We say-- we used to say in-- there's in Hebrew, it happened to Jacob when he left-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] when he ran away from-- from Esau.
On the way, he dreamt that there was a ladder standing on the ground.
And reached up to the sky.
And he saw the angels going up and down.
Of course, the commentators say that these angels were a change of guards.
Those who was with him up to the road there, where Israel was supposed to be-- the future-- finished.
They went back to-- to the sky, and down came another set, which they-- they walked with him further.
But this is just a legend.
The actual meaning is that there were people-- they will say, you are-- if you are on the ground, but your head could reach up to the sky if you are up to it and if you make it this way.
These people that were teaching there-- these people in this-- they were real scholars and dedicated.
And we used to call them 'der himmelmenschen'-- [SPEAKING HEBREW].
It means they were-- you look to you like a normal human being, but their-- their mind, their heads, reached to the skies.
So those were-- those were people who were dedicated to you like-- like your own family.
And so, between the-- the-- the friends that you made and the teachers, they were just like your own family.
Oh, no!
I was a youngster, then!
I was-- I came to this Ostrowca when I was-- in '35, how old was I? 15 years old.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] [SIGHS] And when the Germans came in, when-- when-- when the German occupation started in-- in 1939, I was not even 20 yet.
Just [INAUDIBLE] 20th birthday.

So I was alone, and I-- I became friends with some people in the city, which, on the holidays, they used-- they used to invite me for a meal or a special [INAUDIBLE] in school it was-- you know, the food was not exactly connoisseur, you know, the best food around.
So those people used to sometimes take a youngster and give him a good meal for-- for the holidays.
Especially these people that I knew.
They had no children, so they were glad to have me over for dinner.
So when the war broke out, they asked me to come and stay with them.
So I became a part of this family.
And so I was not married.
And I was too young for that.
No, my Hebrew I learned when I was a-- when I started.
I started out-- the only language that I really enjoyed was Hebrew, because in-- in Yiddish, when we'd-- the teacher or the rabbi or the-- used to speak, and you want to make notation.
And Yiddish is very difficult to make-- to write down.
So, in order to write it down, I wrote in Hebrew, because Hebrew is like shorthand for Yiddish.
They say, you know, [SPEAKING HEBREW].
It is short.
It's like shorthand for Yiddish.
And there I learned my-- my Hebrew, because-- and, of course, all the books that we read was Hebrew.
